title: The New Seismic Discovery Beneath the Surface of Mars
gist: Oxford scientists analyzing NASA InSight seismometer data conclude in Nature Astronomy that the Martian crust is more complex than thought — Mars' stagnant-lid tectonics may still recycle magma like Earth, despite lacking plate tectonics.

insights.claim: University of Oxford scientists, analyzing NASA InSight seismometer data in Nature Astronomy, concluded the Martian crust is far more complex than thought and that Mars may recycle magma despite lacking Earth-style plate tectonics.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Reframes Mars geophysics; relevant to planetary-science instrumentation and future mission rationale.
insights.quote: the Martian crust is a lot more complicated than we thought
insights.locator: Analyzing the data, University of Oxford

insights.claim: Mars has 'stagnant lid' tectonics — a single rocky shell rather than plates floating on magma — which was thought to preclude the magma recycling needed for a complex crust, an assumption the new study overturns.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: A mental model shift in comparative planetology; basic science with no near-term market read.
insights.quote: Mars has “stagnant lid” tectonics characterized by a single rocky shell

insights.claim: NASA's InSight seismometer, landed in 2018, confirmed marsquakes are real and collected four years of subsurface data before dust on its solar panels disabled it in 2022.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Solar-panel dust accumulation remains a recurring failure mode for Mars hardware, a design constraint for mission planners.
insights.quote: a layer of dust covering its solar panels put InSight out of commission in 2022
insights.locator: In 2018, NASA landed the InSight seismometer
